title: Taramasalata
subtitle: Greek smoked cod roe dip
excerpt: The real taramasalata, smoked cod roe blended with olive oil, lemon juice, and a little bread or potato until it becomes pale pink and creamy. Nothing like the supermarket tub.
sourceNotes: Taramasalata derives from the Turkish word for cured fish roe (tarama). The Greek and Turkish mezze version is documented in Claudia Roden, A Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) and Diane Kochilas, The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001). The pale pink colour of homemade versions comes from the roe itself, not artificial colouring.

content.text: Homemade taramasalata is a different thing from the product in a supermarket tub. The supermarket version is pale, thick, and gently fishy; the real one is pale pink, creamy, and sharp with lemon, with the sea-salt savour of the roe coming through clearly. It takes 15 minutes and a food processor.

(smoked cod roe) is sold in small tubs in the chilled aisle of most large supermarkets and in Greek or Middle Eastern delis. The ratio of roe to oil is personal; this recipe gives a balanced result. The bread (or potato for a gluten-free version) serves as an emulsifier and softens the salt of the roe.

items.name: Tarama
items.prepNote: smoked cod roe from the tub

items.name: White bread
items.prepNote: slices, crusts removed, soaked in cold water for 5 minutes and squeezed dry

content.text: Put the tarama and squeezed bread into the food processor. Add the lemon juice and onion if using. Process to a smooth paste.

With the motor running, add the olive oil in a thin, steady stream, as if making mayonnaise. The mixture will turn pale and creamy as the oil emulsifies with the roe. If it splits and looks greasy, add a splash of cold water and blend again. Taste and add more lemon juice or a little cold water to balance.
Transfer to a bowl and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes. The flavour settles and sharpens as it chills. Serve with pitta or crusty bread, olives, and lemon wedges.

items.fix: Add a tablespoon of cold water and blend at high speed to re-emulsify; add the remaining oil more slowly
items.cause: Oil added too fast; the emulsion broke

items.fix: Add more squeezed bread or a peeled, boiled potato (100 g) to dilute the salt; adjust lemon to balance
items.cause: Tarama varies in salinity; some brands are much saltier than others

items.fix: No fix needed; the bright colour indicates a less adulterated product
items.cause: The roe is less processed and more intensely coloured, which is actually preferable

items.fix: Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, blending between additions, until the consistency loosens
items.cause: Too much bread relative to oil

content.text: Taramasalata is eaten throughout the Orthodox Lenten season in Greece, when meat and dairy are avoided. Cod roe and olive oil provide the fat and protein that disappear from the diet. Outside Lent it appears on every mezze spread and is considered as fundamental as tzatziki. The name comes from the Turkish tarama (salted fish roe) and salata (salad); Greek, Turkish, and Levantine versions are all closely related.
